page: Amazon Web Services r5a.xlarge is a memory-optimized instance powered by the AWS Nitro hypervisor. It features an x86_64 architecture with an AMD EPYC 7571 processor operating at 2.5 GHz. The instance provides 4 dedicated vCPUs derived from 2 physical cores with hyper-threading enabled. The memory configuration consists of 32.0 GB of DDR4 RAM running at 3 GHz, yielding an 8.0 GB per core ratio. There is no local NVMe storage or GPU acceleration included, and the baseline network bandwidth is rated at 1 Gbps.

Benchmark results indicate that the r5a.xlarge exhibits lower-tier compute and memory performance. In stress-ng and Geekbench tests, both single-core and multi-core CPU performance fall into the bottom 10% category. Memory bandwidth tests show weak read-only performance in the bottom 25% and poor write performance in the bottom 10%. However, the processor demonstrates average performance in the PassMark CPU encryption test, supported by hardware-accelerated AES and SHA_NI instruction sets.

The primary tradeoff of the r5a.xlarge is its high memory-to-vCPU ratio paired with modest processing speeds. While it offers a cost-efficient entry point for memory-bound workloads that do not require high compute density, it is less suited for CPU-heavy tasks, as evidenced by poor scores in software compilation, image processing, and database operations. This instance is best suited for memory-intensive applications with low CPU utilization, such as caching layers, basic Redis deployments, or lightweight memory-bound background workers.
description: Amazon Web Services r5a.xlarge is a memory-optimized instance featuring 4 dedicated vCPUs and 32.0 GB of DDR4 RAM. Powered by an AMD EPYC 7571 processor running at 2.5 GHz on the Nitro hypervisor, this instance provides an 8.0 GB memory-to-core ratio. It lacks local storage and GPU accelerators, and has a baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps. Performance benchmarks place the instance in the lower tiers, with stress-ng CPU and memory write bandwidth scoring in the bottom 10%, though it achieves average results in PassMark encryption tests due to hardware-accelerated AES and SHA_NI support. The r5a.xlarge represents a cost-efficient option for memory-intensive workloads with low compute demands, such as caching layers, Redis deployments, and lightweight memory-bound applications.
